speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Two more tapes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As many as it takes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As many as it takes. Tape three with Betty Daniel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk louder. Speak up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about violence that you might have seen. And you talked about your sister and the German shepherd-- being afraid of dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other violence did you see at Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I personally did not see the violence. It was my sister. But she told that-- that that it was. But otherwise in Bergen-Belson, No. I don't recollect anything. We all were very down spiritually-- mentally, I would say. Very down-- depressed, every one of us. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any stories that the other prisoners talked about-- about what was going on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember myself-- I remember stepping over dead bodies. That what I remember. And it became sort of like a-- I would-- I didn't-- no reaction. No reaction. I don't know how I would react now if I would have to-- to face-- to face that. But at that time, it was sort of like-- maybe it was the attitude. Depression-- by seeing somebody dead, and to step over. Which was not right, but we had no choice. Until somebody that was assigned to take him away, and to put them which-- to put them-- to-- to-- in piles. That was Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have to put the bodies in piles? Did you have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't pile, but I just stepped over if I saw a body.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bergen-Belsen, were there any other painful moments that you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the liberation, no, no, no. I don't remember. No. No. Anything else you want to talk about-- about Bergen-Belsen, about your time there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, up to the liberation-- the liberation I remember very well. My sister was in very bad condition. She was laying on the cement. And I was walking around, and all of a sudden I see at the gate-- I see a tank with soldiers. And it started. And with my little English, I-- I could talk with them. And they told us that they were liberators.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was April 14 or the 15, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country where are they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- I have a mixed emotions. From some, I heard American soldiers, and from some I hear English soldiers. So what's the difference? They were liberators.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of them-- one of them gave me an orange. And this orange is in my mind. And it's sort of like I saw a star falling down from the heaven. [LAUGHS] And I ran was this orange to my sister to share, as usually I did. No response. Of course, that was very sad. And that was the liberators. And it was right away, as I remember, we got some cleaning. And this is in my mind-- DDT. They-- we had some powder. They sprayed us with DDT. Of course, we gave up all our clothes. And we got some other clothes that cleansed us up. And since then, I can say honestly I haven't seen a lice on my face-- on my body. And I keep clean. [LAUGHS] But the DDT is in my mind. That was the liberation. And I survived. My sister was taken to-- to a hospital. And I was around. But it was a big commotion.

speaker: interviewee
text: How-- somehow, I lost my sister. I didn't know where she was. And it was a very big disappointment for me, until one day, I [INAUDIBLE] the survivors [INAUDIBLE] have you seen so-and-so. I am so-and-so. Which barrack are you? And one day, I heard that-- you know, I saw your sister there and there? I said, oh really? Where is she? So I ran. So we found each other again. Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in a-- in a different division of the typhus from the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you found--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another reunion with my sister. Because I was really worried, in the condition that she was, whether she didn't pass away. But she survived. And somebody told her that I am here-- here. So we-- so since then, we got taken to a convalescent home in Germany. Not too many of them-- there were some 18 girls and about a few men. So it was like a border town to the Dutch border. That time, we were asked to go to Sweden. But I think as I remember, there was a choice, whether you like it or not. So we were sent to the small-- maybe it is a kind of sickness what one had too. I had pleurisy. And my sister had typhus. So we both recovered. And that hospital over there-- we were in a town where the mayor of the town heard that some survivors from concentration camps are there. And as a treat, he took us to the department store and tried to clothe us-- to give us some new clothes. That was a real treat for us. And we chosed clothes. And I have until today-- I keep as a-- as a momento-- I keeped the blouse which I have-- the-- the first blouse after the camp. It's a nice blouse. It's a-- it's a jersey. And I have it. And I got to-- a new, a raincoat. It was polka dots. And it looks like until today I like polka dots. So one of the girls liked what I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told her, you like it? You can have it. So it looks to me that I-- it remained in me to sharing, whether I shared with my sister or shared with someone else. And I gave her the raincoat. Of course, I got another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, one of the men stood behind. He says, wowee. She has a good heart. She doesn't have anything and she gives it away. So he sort of like introduced himself and wrote a poem to-- to be my friend. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have the poem still. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: First tell us who that man was. Tell us who that man was.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man was-- is-- was my husband. Jacob Daniel. And he was a survivor from Warsaw ghetto. So he couldn't find anyone of his seven brothers and one sister. So he was the only one survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was in the convalescent hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was in that convalescent hospital, recovering. Actually, I think his stomach couldn't take the food-- what-- to the normal life. So not being able to eat, there was that time, another man who introduced him to the moonshine-- alcohol. So they started to drink. So as soon as I got into the picture, he stopped drinking. And I tried to persuade him. So that was the introduction. The poem from him-- and me to him was introduction to healthy food and to give up drinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the poem say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the poem says-- he expressed himself that I had very sad eyes. Sad-- why you so sad? The depression-- it was a depression, certain depression that I had, worried-- whatever we went through. And it reflected in my eyes. So he asked in his-- in the poem, he asked why I am so depressed. And he is very sad that I don't react different-- sort of like willingness to return friendship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But as it warmed up a little bit, actually he presented me this-- he says that-- he didn't say that he wrote the poem. He wrote that he knew that I know English. And he has family, and he would like to learn English-- whether I could correct it. So I said that I'm not qualified as good to correct a language that other man-- [? Leon ?] who was there too-- he can correct it better.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, oh no. He cannot correct this letter. Just read it. So as I opened it, I said, well he's right. A poem-- this was written to me. And I'm sending him to correct this. Sort of like it was a pretext.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was an introduction, and I think it changed my life. So we still recovered, until later on, my sister met her boyfriend. He was in a camp. He survived. And they met together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we read the poem, before we continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to have my glasses though. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have to have my glasses. They are in the kitchen on the table. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] By the way, this poem, I framed it. It was all my things here, which I survived. It's my DP card.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can go over that in the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go over that and your photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have a-- we have a rest now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're just gonna go through the poem. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just the poem, right now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You understand it over there. Betty, so-- that was beautiful. How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you understand that, it's beautiful. It's a beautiful poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the poem changed your life at that time. How did it change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed my life that I found someone-- without knowing and deciding-- that this will be in my future. I didn't have anyone to ask advice. Mom, should I marry? Daddy, who is he? Whether he's from Warsaw-- who was he? All that I knew was that he had a family and friends, and they wanted him, and in Mexico too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you met your husband, at that time, what happened next? You were in the hospital. Where did you go next? Did you try and return to your-- your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My sister was, at that time, in a DP camp because she joined her boyfriend-- still from the hometown that he survived. So we went over there. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Landsberg. So we both took a train. We were released from the hospital. And we went on our own-- I think on our own-- we went to Landsberg to my sister. At that time, my sister was in DP camp. So I was-- I was together with her. But my husband, at that time, Jacob, at the time, he didn't intend to remain in Europe. He wanted to be with the family. And we received a letter from his aunt and uncle, from Paris, that they survive all by hiding in the countryside with some French people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they promised us and mentioned-- until today, I have the letters too-- they say and bring Betty too. So I was included already. And without knowing, my sister planned the wedding for herself. So I got [INAUDIBLE] now that she wanted to make a double wedding. And here, someone came-- that it's easy to go to France. So we went, smuggled ourself without passports, to go and to cross the border in [INAUDIBLE]. So we crossed. And all of a sudden, we-- everything went smooth. And we see a railroad station. So we went into the railroad station to the train. And over there, the French police caught us. We had no papers. So both of us, and another cousin, were jailed and faced a trial. And then I-- How long did you spend in the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family-- family came and vouched for us, that we won't be a burden to the government. It was a little restriction after the war. They were afraid for spies or whatever. But because we didn't have any false papers and the family came to vouch for us, we were allowed to go into Paris. And that was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, how long did you spend in the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: One month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like we-- it was Passover. And some Jews from the gathering-- from the Federation in [INAUDIBLE] broke us some matzos. So it was a little-- it was another experience. But it paid off because we were not sent back to Germany. And then I-- my sister got married without me. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before we get to your marriage, what-- did you find out what happened to your family members after the war? Did you try to find out about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being in Bergen-Belsen after the liberation, we met different people and everyone searched for everyone else. I knew that one brother passed away-- got killed. But the other brother-- I didn't-- I knew which camp he was, but later on, I found out that this camp was very, very, very bad camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Gross-Rosen. And Theresienstadt too. So we didn't here anything. Occasionally we heard from someone that they were together with him in a camp. But otherwise, no. The parents-- we knew that they were in-- in Auschwitz. And from 1943 to '45-- '43 was the worst year in-- in Auschwitz. The crematoriums were very busy that time. So there was a-- and my father was-- was not very young-- he wasn't old. But he wasn't in any camp either. So we gather that he must have been cremated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about returning to your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, no. My husband especially said that he doesn't want to be in Europe anymore. So he aimed to go with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So getting back to-- you just left the prison. The relatives-- your husband's relatives got you out. Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Paris-- from Paris the family from Mexico wanted us. And my husband wanted to go to a-- to a warm climate country. Because his-- his father passed away. There was a heart condition. A heart condition. So he was afraid that maybe he has a heart condition too. So he said he doesn't want to be in any cold climate living. So Mexico was very well, and we were wanted over there. But it was very hard to get into Mexico. So we went to Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married, before--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got married in Paris, in '46. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, in 1946, we got married. My husband-- one of my [? landsmans, ?] from the town needed the worker. My husband had some knowledge in the garment industry. So-- but never had any sewing machine operated on a motor. So after the war, there were already motorized machines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the one, my [? landsman, ?] he needed a worker. So he said to my husband, how about you come up? And that was a very-- the first home for me. To be in it, while my husband was working there. The surrounding was very good. His wife told me, bring yourself something in the kitchen, and prepare food, and everything. And they even prepared the reception when we get married. And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Paris. Yes. And until today, I am really grateful to them. And then when the papers came, we had to go through a-- a visa-- a transit visa. At that time, they couldn't be from Paris-- Paris, Havana. I had to go through New York and transfer. But I was embarrassed-- pregnant with my daughter. And I had to leave. So we went to Cuba. So in Cuba, my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your life for us in Cuba.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first year when we arrived in Cuba, besides the point, I got relatives. My aunt-- my mother's aunt-- sent me beautiful baby clothes from Chicago-- helped us. And the Mexican family helped us. My sister-- and when my daughter was born, there was very-- my husband was very depressed. Why? Because he-- he wanted to be independent. He didn't like to depend on anyone. Here we are in a climate-- very sticky-- but a very good climate. So he had no right to work. If he got some work, they-- he had to be hidden-- not to find him. But one day, after he was peddling-- tried selling little-- on the street, one day somebody knocked at the door and offered us a beautiful position, trusting us a-- a business to overtake in a country store. It was a beautiful opportunity. But we were the only Jews there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were asked whether we would take it. So my husband says, listen, the baby is small. I have time to think about this. So we took-- and those were the beautiful four years of us in Cuba. But while the first year it was so depressing, I registered for the quota to go to the United States, where my sister already was. So during that time in Cuba, you were in Havana?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one year in Havana and four years in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your experiences with other people? Did people want to talk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Jews. And luckily, there was one from Warsaw that knew my husband's family. And there were some-- some survivors over there too. So we talked about this, yes. But still not as open as-- as we would like to-- to get it out. The depression was still in there. But when my daughter was born, there was a happiness-- that she will bear the name of our mothers. It happened-- my brother's name was Hannah and my husband's mother's name was Hannah. So she was the name. And that was a little princess that-- that [? lifed ?] up our life. And being in a countryside where my husband was in his glory, operating a business, trusting, confidence that that was bestowed on us. It was beautiful years. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my son was born. So again, the name of my husband's father, Simon. So that was happiness too. So it was a happy five-- five years I would say in Cuba. But the quota came up and we had to decide whether to remain in Cuba or to come to the United States. It was a big decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband said, here we have to face a decision. I tried not to go to the United States. But it looks like my destiny is in the United States. So we decided. And when we arrived here in the United States, my husband said, throw away the suitcase. I don't want to travel anymore. That's it. What year was that, that you arrived in the United--?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, my sister was here in Los Angeles already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1953. I arrived with my daughter. She was three and a half year old-- almost five-- almost five and my son was a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here to [INAUDIBLE]. We left Havana-- Miami-- and Miami, Los Angeles. And Los Angeles here-- I came to my sister. And I had here my aunt-- one aunt living. And it was sort of like-- I knew that that's it. That is the destiny-- and to make a living here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your husband do? And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, he had distilled experience in the garment industry. He went to the garment industry as an operator. He worked on two or three jobs to maintain us. I was home with the children. And then, after working I think a year or two in the shop, the owner wanted to retire. And my husband says, let's buy the shop. So I started to go to work with him together. And we both operated the shop for 10 years, until he got sick.
